agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order lifecycle management covering placement, invoicing, and cancellation in a system where finance requires invoice immutability and operations invoices at placement time. A product manager wants cancellation available up until shipment.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A commercial commitment by a customer to purchase goods, progressing through placement, invoicing, and shipment stages."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, reversing the customer's commitment. Business disagreement exists on when this is permitted."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document recording the obligation to pay. Finance considers this irreversible once created."},{"term":"Invoicing","definition":"The act of generating an invoice for an order. Operations performs this at placement time to lock in pricing."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer. PM considers this the cancellation cutoff."},{"term":"Placement","definition":"The moment an order is confirmed by the customer and enters the system. Operations invoices at this point."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a PM-stated business policy about the cancellation window. However, it directly contradicts BR-2 and BR-3 when combined.","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has not yet shipped, when cancellation is requested, then the order is cancelled. (BLOCKED — contradicts BR-2 + BR-3)"},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled; invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats invoice immutability as a hard financial integrity constraint — reversing an invoice has legal, tax, and audit implications. This is a data/state integrity rule.","testable_assertion":"Given an order with an associated invoice, when cancellation is requested, then the cancellation is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy decision about when invoicing occurs. It could be changed to invoice later (e.g., at shipment), but currently it is active policy.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, when placement completes, then an invoice exists for that order immediately."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer places an order and immediately requests cancellation","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — under BR-1 this should succeed (not yet shipped), but under BR-2 + BR-3 this must fail (already invoiced at placement)."},{"scenario":"Order placed but shipment delayed for days","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — PM expects a cancellation window exists; Finance says it closed at placement."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment scenario","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — if part of an order ships, cancellation scope is unclear even without the contradiction."},{"scenario":"System failure prevents invoice generation at placement","expected_behavior":"If invoicing fails at placement, does the order exist without an invoice? If so, it would be cancellable under all three rules, but this may indicate a broken invariant for BR-3."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High. If we follow Finance (no cancellation post-invoice), customers effectively cannot cancel any order, which may increase chargebacks, disputes, and customer churn. If we follow PM (allow cancellation pre-ship), we must solve invoice reversal, impacting accounting integrity, tax reporting, and audit compliance.","operational":"The operations team invoices early to lock pricing. Any resolution that delays invoicing changes their pricing-lock workflow. Any resolution that introduces credit notes adds accounting process overhead.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders that haven't shipped. An inability to cancel would be a significant UX and trust issue, likely increasing support volume.","risk":"Implementing any single interpretation without resolving the contradiction guarantees a violation of at least one stakeholder's stated constraint. Legal/tax risk if invoices are reversed without proper credit note processes. Customer retention risk if cancellation is blocked.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders at all under current Finance + Ops rules, leading to frustration, chargebacks, and potential loss of trust."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Any solution enabling cancellation requires addressing invoice reversal — possibly via credit notes — adding complexity to reconciliation and tax reporting."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"If invoicing is decoupled from placement to resolve the conflict, their pricing-lock mechanism must be redesigned."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"The cancellation feature as specified cannot ship until this contradiction is resolved."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the frontline burden of whichever policy is implemented — either explaining why cancellation is impossible or handling a new credit note process."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The three requirements form a logical contradiction: BR-3 (invoice at placement) + BR-2 (invoiced orders cannot be cancelled) = orders can never be cancelled, which directly negates BR-1 (cancel before shipment). No amount of clever implementation can satisfy all three simultaneously. Confidence is low because the spec is internally inconsistent and no acceptance criteria can be defined until stakeholders resolve the conflict.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and unambiguous — it is a logical impossibility, not an interpretation issue.","Invoice immutability is a legitimate financial integrity constraint.","Invoicing at placement is current operational policy."],"low_confidence_areas":["Which stakeholder's constraint should yield — this is a business decision, not a technical one.","Whether the organization has a credit note or invoice voiding process that could bridge the gap.","Whether 'invoiced' in Finance's constraint means the same thing as 'invoiced' in Operations' process."],"assumptions":["All three statements accurately reflect current organizational policy as understood by each team.","There is no existing credit note or invoice reversal process that Finance omitted from their constraint.","'Invoice' means the same thing across both the Finance and Operations teams."]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements are mutually exclusive. Operations invoices at placement (BR-3). Finance says invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (BR-2). Combined, these mean no order can ever be cancelled after placement. This directly contradicts the PM requirement that orders can be cancelled before shipment (BR-1). All three cannot be true simultaneously.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Delay invoicing until shipment. Orders are cancellable pre-ship. Operations must find another mechanism to lock pricing (e.g., a price-lock hold that is not an invoice).","B: Invoice at placement but introduce a credit note / invoice voiding process. Cancellation triggers a credit note rather than deleting the invoice, preserving Finance's audit trail while enabling the PM's cancellation window.","C: Accept that orders cannot be cancelled post-placement. Redefine the PM requirement to only allow cancellation during a brief pre-invoice window (effectively zero under current ops).","D: Distinguish between a 'proforma invoice' (issued at placement for pricing lock) and a 'final invoice' (issued at shipment). Only the final invoice triggers the no-cancellation rule."],"recommendation":"Option B or D. Option B (credit notes) is the most common industry resolution — it preserves invoice immutability while enabling cancellation via a compensating financial transaction. Option D may also work if Operations' 'invoice' is really a proforma/price-lock and not a true tax invoice. Recommend a joint meeting between PM, Finance, and Operations to align on terminology and select a resolution before any implementation begins."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot implement order cancellation as specified","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is logically impossible when BR-2 and BR-3 are both enforced. Implementing BR-1 violates BR-2; enforcing BR-2 + BR-3 nullifies BR-1."}]}
